EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A swell from a powerful low far northwest of the Aloha State generated surf of 15 to 25 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 10 to 20 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai; and 6 to 9 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii.  Beaches were closed for a time on Kauai because of the high surf conditions.  Lifeguards were busy elsewhere with rescues and keeping inexperienced surfers out of the water.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

A long period swell from the southern hemisphere generated surf of 6 to 12 feet along the south-facing shores of all the main Hawaiian Islands.  In a few instances, water from the high surf flowed over adjacent roads and deposited sand and other debris.||Lifeguards were busy throughout the episode, rescuing more than 100 surfers and swimmers, and issuing several hundreds of warnings.  One surfer died from injuries suffered at Ala Moana Bowls on Oahu on June 6th, while another male on the same day on Oahu sustained serious injuries while surfing at Sandy Beach.  There were no reports of serious property damage.

A swell from a powerful low far northwest of the islands generated surf of 15 to 30 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 10 to 20 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 10 to 15 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii; and 8 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of Lanai and Kahoolawe.||Two men were swept away by the large waves on the north shore of Kauai in the later afternoon of the 18th.  Lifeguards on Oahu, alone, accounted for more than 2000 safety actions with this high surf episode.  Many beaches were closed for a time because of the rough conditions, and several roadways near the shoreline on the individual isles became covered with debris from waves breaking beyond the beach areas.  The costs of any property damage were not available.

Lifeguards rescued several people in trouble and warned many others to stay away from the rough surf if they were not experienced in such conditions. No serious injuries or property damage were reported.  This is the continuation of an episode that began at the end of December 2012.

A series of swells from the southern hemisphere generated surf of 6 to 10 feet along the south shores of all islands.  Lifeguards were busy throughout the high surf episode.  They provided many rescues, and warnings to inexperienced swimmers and surfers.  On Maui, with the high surf, three sailing vessels broke free from their moorings and washed aground near Mala Wharf in Lahaina.  The cost of damages was not available.  There were no reports of serious injuries.

On Maui, with the high surf, three sailing vessels broke free from their moorings and washed aground near Mala Wharf in Lahaina.  The cost of damages was not available.  There were no reports of serious injuries.

A surface trough and upper low combined to bring heavy rain to the islands at the end of May.  The showers primarily caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.||On the 28th on Oahu, however, the rainfall was intense enough to overflow the banks of the Kalihi Stream on private property due to clogged culverts.  This overflow did not affect public areas, though.  Also, four teenagers were caught in the swollen stream for a time before they were able to make their way out of danger.||By the end of the episode, fortunately, no serious injuries or property damage were reported.

A long period swell from the southern hemisphere generated surf of 6 to 12 feet along the south-facing shores of all the main Hawaiian Islands.  In a few instances, water from the high surf flowed over adjacent roads and deposited sand and other debris.||Lifeguards were busy throughout the episode, rescuing more than 100 surfers and swimmers, and issuing several hundreds of warnings.  One surfer died from injuries suffered at Ala Moana Bowls on Oahu on June 6th, while another male on the same day on Oahu sustained serious injuries while surfing at Sandy Beach.  There were no reports of serious property damage.

Upper troughing ahead of a cold front led to heavy rain over parts of the Big Island of Hawaii and Oahu.  The precipitation caused small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

Heavy rain, from lingering convective cloudiness after daytime heating, fell over southwest parts of East Maui.  The rain produced ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  No significant property damage or injuries were reported.

A surface trough and upper low combined to bring heavy rain to the islands at the end of May.  The showers primarily caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.||On the 28th on Oahu, however, the rainfall was intense enough to overflow the banks of the Kalihi Stream on private property due to clogged culverts.  This overflow did not affect public areas, though.  Also, four teenagers were caught in the swollen stream for a time before they were able to make their way out of danger.||By the end of the episode, fortunately, no serious injuries or property damage were reported.

A swell from a powerful low far northwest of the Aloha State generated surf of 15 to 25 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 10 to 20 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai; and 6 to 9 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii. A fire blackened 50 acres of fallow agricultural land near Whitmore Village in central Oahu.  The dry brush fire came close to a section of Kamehameha Highway, which was closed for more than hour in the afternoon while fire crews worked to contain the blaze.  The cause of the fire was unknown.  There were no serious injuries or property damage.

A prescribed burn by the Army became unmanageable, and spread, as winds increased in central Oahu near the Helemano Military Reservation.  The fire eventually engulfed more than 200 acres of brush.  Sections of Kamehameha Highway near the blaze had to be closed for a time, up to four hours along one stretch.  However, no significant property damage or injuries were reported.

A blaze in West Oahu blackened 40 acres of dry brush on state land in Maili, and came within 100 feet of several homes.  Some residents left voluntarily as the flames approached their area.  Fortunately, firefighters were able to contain the blaze before it could damage any houses.  There were no serious injuries or property damage.  The cause of the fire was being investigated.

A fire burned 42 acres of brush near Wailua on the windward coast of Kauai.  The blaze was near the Aston Aloha Beach Resort and the Kaha Lani Resort condominiums.  However, neither site was significantly affected by the fire.  The cause of the blaze was unknown.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

Instability generated by an upper trough northwest of the Aloha State helped to initiate heavy showers over the leeward slopes of Mauna Loa on the Big Island. A weak surface trough near Molokai, in combination with a northeast-moving upper system, helped trigger heavy rain over parts of Oahu.  The showers caused small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.

A strong upper trough moving from the northwest re-energized the remnants of an old front.  Heavy rain drenched the Garden Isle of Kauai, producing flash flooding in some instances.  The inclement weather spread to other isles as well.  However, there were no reports of significant injuries.  The costs of any property damages were not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kauai law enforcement and emergency manager reported the closing of the highway at the Hanalei Bridge due to high water reaching the road.

However, there were no reports of significant injuries.  The costs of any property damages were not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lihue Police reported runoff and debris at Kapahi Park from the Kapaa Stream.

A light east to southeast wind flow, in combination with locally unstable conditions, generated heavy rain over parts of windward Big Island.  The precipitation caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.

Remnant moisture from a dissipated front fueled heavy showers over parts of Maui and Oahu.  Some of the showers were intense enough to produce flash flooding in a windward section of Oahu near Waikane.  There were no serious injuries or property damage.

Winds in excess of 58 mph affected portions of Maui County and the Big Island of Hawaii.  No significant injuries were reported.  The high winds caused property damage in isolated locales, though the costs were not available.
A weather system moving from the west affected the Garden Isle of Kauai, the island  of Oahu, and Molokai.  There were no reports of serious injuries.  The cost of damages was not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported in a building at Barking Sands Pacific Missile Range Facility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather system moving from the west affected the Garden Isle of Kauai, the island  of Oahu, and Molokai.  There were no reports of serious injuries.  The cost of damages was not available.

Upper level instability near and east of the Aloha State enhanced showers that affected Maui and the Island of Hawaii.  No significant property damage or injuries were reported.

A series of swells from strong lows far northwest of Hawaii caused surf of 15 to 30 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and along the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 10 to 20 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai, 8 to 15 feet along the north-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii; and 6 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island.||Lifeguards were busy throughout the state issuing warnings to beach-goers and providing advice to surfers, along with performing occasional rescues of those in trouble in the water.||Because of beach erosion, several homes on the north shore of Oahu were threatened during this series of high surf events.  The cost of damages was not available.  No serious injuries were reported.

Heavy rain and thunderstorms affected much of the Big Island of Hawaii at the end of December.  Flash flooding occurred, along with falling hail and possibly a microburst from a thunderstorm.  There were no reports of serious injuries.  The cost of damages was not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 19 was closed between mile markers 34 and 37 due to severe flooding.  Also, Pohakea Mauka Road was washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An advancing cold front and upper trough brought heavy rain and thunderstorms to portions of Kauai and Oahu.  The systems continued to affect the isles into early December.  No significant injuries were reported.  The total cost of damages was not available.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was felled by strong wind just before the reservoir in Kokee State Park on Kauai.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding reported at Ainako Avenue near Kaumana Drive in Hilo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A swell from powerful low far northwest of the islands produced surf of 20 to 30 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 15 to 25 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii; and 10 to 15 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, Lanai, and Kahoolawe.||Lifeguards were busy issuing more than 4800 warnings to beach-goers, and rescuing or assisting more than 50 people on Oahu alone during this episode.  One woman and a man were injured by the high surf as well, and many beaches on the west side of the Big Island were closed because of the huge surf.||Also on the west side of the Big Island, two boating facilities were damaged.  The loading dock at Puako Boat Ramp failed and collapsed into the water, and the launch ramp at the Kawaihae Small Boat Harbor was engulfed by high waves that snapped several planks from the structure.  The cost of damages was being calculated and not yet available.

A series of swells from strong lows far northwest of Hawaii caused surf of 15 to 30 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and along the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 10 to 20 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai, 8 to 15 feet along the north-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii; and 6 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island.||Lifeguards were busy throughout the state issuing warnings to beach-goers and providing advice to surfers, along with performing occasional rescues of those in trouble in the water.||Because of beach erosion, several homes on the north shore of Oahu were threatened during this series of high surf events.  The cost of damages was not available.  No serious injuries were reported.

A weather system moving from the west affected the Garden Isle of Kauai, the island  of Oahu, and Molokai.  There were no reports of serious injuries.  The cost of damages was not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was felled near Barking Sands Pacific Missile Range Facility. The exact time of the event was uncertain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and thunderstorms affected much of the Big Island of Hawaii at the end of December.  Flash flooding occurred, along with falling hail and possibly a microburst from a thunderstorm.  There were no reports of serious injuries.  The cost of damages was not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire officials reported a gust of wind damaged a roof on a home on Hoopuni Street in Hilo.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall led to Pohakea Mauka Road being washed out.  Time estimated by radar and rain gauge data.

A swell from a powerful low north of the islands, in combination with a strong high far to the northwest, generated surf of 20 to 30 feet along the north-facing shores, and 10 to 20 feet along the east-facing shores, of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island of Hawaii.||A surfer was lost on the 13th in the churning waters on the north shore of Oahu at Chun's Reef.  A search for the 32-year-old male was unsuccessful.||On the Valley Isle of Maui, the parking and pavilion areas of Baldwin Park in Paia were closed due to flooding from the high surf wash up on the 13th.  Bayfront Highway on the Big Island was also closed for a time because of the unusually high surf.||The costs of any damages from the high surf were not available.

An upper level low north of the islands combined with a surface trough and shear line to produce heavy rain and flash flooding over parts of the Aloha State.  There were no reports of serious injuries.  The cost of damages was not available.

A low north of the Aloha State generated surf of 10 to 18 feet along the north-facing shores, and 6 to 10 feet along the east-facing shores, of all the islands except Lanai.  There were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of Bayfront Highway was closed in Hilo in the evening hours on the 10th because of debris from high surf, high tide, and runoff from rainfall.

An upper level low north of the islands combined with a surface trough and shear line to produce heavy rain and flash flooding over parts of the Aloha State.  There were no reports of serious injuries.  The cost of damages was not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused the Hanalei River to overflow its banks along Kuhio Highway near the Hanalei Bridge in north Kauai.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flossie, downgraded to tropical depression status as it neared Maui at 1700 HST on the 29th, affected the state from July 28th through the 30th, though high surf continued through the 31st on the Big Island of Hawaii.  The system brought surf of 8 to 15 feet along east-facing shores of most islands, thunderstorms, heavy rain and a flash flood, and wind.  One man was injured by lightning on Maui, and power outages occurred on the Big Island, Maui, and Molokai.  The cost of damages was not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 35-foot tree blown down by Momona Road in Kurtistown.

An upper low just north of the islands induced heavy rain and thunderstorms over the Garden Isle of Kauai, and heavy showers over Oahu.  The rain produced ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries, though children in schools in northern parts of Kauai were let out early as a precaution in the event that the Hanalei Bridge would be closed due to flooding.  Also on Kauai, debris flows occurred along Kuhio Highway near the Wainiha and Lumahai rivers, limiting the roadway to one lane for a time.

A swell from a strong low far northwest of the islands generated surf of 15 to 20 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and 10 to 15 feet along the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui.  On the 21st, three individuals were injured when they were swept away on a wave from the Shark's Cove reef area on Oahu's North Shore.  Two of the people, women aged 19 and 20, suffered serious head injuries and multiple abrasions.  An 18-year-old man sustained minor injuries.  Ocean Safety officials were also busy with other rescues, assists, and preventive actions.  No significant property damage was reported.

An advancing cold front and upper trough brought heavy rain, thunderstorms, and/or flash flooding  to portions of Kauai, Oahu, and Molokai.  The total cost of damages was not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of Menehune Road was closed due to debris - large boulders, tree branches, and mud - covering the roadway.  Also, boulders damaged a water main.

Heavy rainfall from trade wind dynamics occurred over portions of northern and eastern Maui.  No significant property damage or injuries were reported.

Upper level instability produced heavy rain over parts of the Big Island of Hawaii and Oahu.  There was ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  No significant property damage or injuries were reported.

A front moving slowly from the west and northwest triggered heavy rain over the isles from Kauai to Molokai.  Most of the showers produced small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.||One period of rain, however, brought flash flooding to a portion of windward Oahu.  Several cars were caught in the high water along Kamehameha Highway the night of the event, though the cost of any damages was not available.  There were no serious injuries reported.
Upper level instability near and east of the Aloha State enhanced showers that affected Maui and the Island of Hawaii.  No significant property damage or injuries were reported.

A strong upper trough moving from the northwest re-energized the remnants of an old front.  Heavy rain drenched the Garden Isle of Kauai, producing flash flooding in some instances.  The inclement weather spread to other isles as well.  However, there were no reports of significant injuries.  The costs of any property damages were not available.
